findings.description: Full card number and CVV are written to application logs at INFO level, a direct PCI-DSS violation (CVV must never be persisted anywhere) exposing cardholder data to anyone with log access.
charge_order never checks whether the order is already 'paid', so a retry or concurrent call charges the same card again (no idempotency guard).
The success UPDATE sets status and tx_id but never sets paid_at, so monthly_revenue_report (which filters on strftime of paid_at) silently excludes every order paid through this code path.
refund marks the order 'refunded' in the database but never calls the payment gateway, so no money is actually returned to the customer.
If the UPDATE or commit fails after gateway.charge succeeds, the customer is charged but the order is never recorded as paid, with no compensation, retry, or reconciliation path.
Monetary values are represented as binary floats end-to-end (unit_price, totals), inviting representation/rounding errors; Decimal or integer cents should be used for money.
compute_order_total never validates discount_pct (or item quantity/unit_price) ranges, so discount_pct > 100 or negative values silently produce negative or inflated totals.

revenue_cents is computed from raw unit_price * quantity, ignoring the per-order discount_pct (and differing tax treatment) applied at charge time, so reported revenue does not match amounts actually charged.

refund's read-then-update is not atomic and the UPDATE lacks an 'AND status = 'paid'' guard with rowcount check, so concurrent refund calls can both pass the status check (double refund once a real gateway call exists).
monthly_revenue_report runs one order_items query per paid order (N+1 pattern); a single JOIN with SUM/GROUP BY would produce the report in one query.
Wrapping paid_at in strftime() makes the predicate non-sargable (no index use, full scan); a half-open range comparison on paid_at would be index-friendly.
int(round(total * 100)) applies Python's banker's rounding to an unrounded float total, so half-cent boundaries can round to even unexpectedly, occasionally shifting the charged amount by one cent.
The stub transaction id built from salted Python hash() is neither deterministic across processes (PYTHONHASHSEED) as the comment claims nor unique, since identical last-4 digits and amount collide.
year/month are never validated, so out-of-range input like month=13 silently returns an empty report instead of raising an error.
Each operation opens a fresh SQLite connection with default busy timeout and no WAL/pragma setup, so concurrent charge/refund traffic can hit 'database is locked' errors.
The bare dict return annotation on monthly_revenue_report hides the report's fixed key set; a TypedDict or dataclass would document and type-check the shape.
